title: A Time Series is Worth 64 Words: Long-term Forecasting with Transformers (PatchTST)
authors: Yuqi Nie (Princeton); Nam H. Nguyen, Phanwadee Sinthong, Jayant Kalagnanam (IBM Research)
tokenization_method: Subseries-level PATCHING + CHANNEL-INDEPENDENCE. (1) Each univariate channel x^(i) (length L) is instance-normalized (RevIN-style zero-mean/unit-std per instance) and segmented into patches of length P with stride S, giving N = floor((L-P)/S) + 2 patches (the +2 from padding S copies of the last value before patching). (2) Each P-length patch is a token: a single trainable linear projection W_p in R^{D x P} maps it to a D-dim embedding, and a learnable additive position encoding W_pos in R^{D x N} is added: x_d^(i) = W_p x_p^(i) + W_pos. (3) A vanilla Transformer encoder (multi-head self-attention + FFN, with BatchNorm instead of LayerNorm) runs over the N patch tokens of ONE channel at a time; ALL channels share the SAME embedding weights and the SAME Transformer weights, but are processed independently (no cross-channel attention). (4) Output: flatten the N D-dim representations and a linear head predicts the T-step horizon for that channel. Multivariate output is the concatenation of M independent channel forecasts.
feature_types_handled: numerical / temporal ONLY. Input is multivariate continuous time series (M numeric channels). No categorical features, no high-cardinality fields, no static covariates, no explicit missing-value handling. Each channel is one univariate real-valued series.
token_granularity: temporal patch: one token = a contiguous P-length window of a SINGLE channel's time series (default P=16). Sequence length seen by attention = N ~= L/S patches per channel (e.g. L=512, P=16, S=8 -> 64 tokens, hence '64 words'; L=336, P=16, S=8 -> 42 tokens for PatchTST/42). NOT per-feature, NOT per-timestep, NOT per-value.
numerical_strategy: Aggregate-then-linearly-project: P raw (instance-normalized) scalar values per patch are stacked into a P-vector and projected by a single shared linear map W_p to D dims. No piecewise-linear, no periodic/Fourier, no binning, no per-feature learned vector. The 'richness' comes from grouping consecutive timesteps (local semantics) rather than from a fancy per-scalar embedding. Instance normalization (per-series mean/std, restored at output) is the key preprocessing that handles distribution shift.
categorical_strategy: none — the model has no categorical pathway at all.
temporal_handling: PATCHING is the temporal tokenizer: consecutive timesteps are aggregated into subseries patches so each token carries local temporal semantics (vs point-wise tokens). Temporal order across patches is encoded by a learnable additive position embedding. Longer history is affordable because patching cuts token count by ~S, letting L grow (paper shows L=96->336 cuts case-study MSE 0.518->0.397).
scales_to_many_features: Scales in SEQUENCE LENGTH (timesteps), NOT in feature/channel count, and the scaling story is the opposite of what this topic needs. Attention is O(N^2) where N is patches PER CHANNEL (~L/S), so patching gives a quadratic memory/compute reduction by factor S in the time dimension (case study: w/ vs w/o patch -> 22x speedup on Traffic, 19x Electricity, 4x Weather at L=336). For the CHANNEL dimension, channel-independence makes cost LINEAR in M (each of M channels runs the same shared model independently) — Traffic has M=862 channels and it trains fine. BUT this is sequential/batched univariate processing, NOT joint tokenization of many heterogeneous features; there is no cross-feature attention and no mixed-type token stream. So 'scales to 862 channels' is real but is NOT the '70+ heterogeneous mixed-type features in one token sequence' the topic targets.
downstream_model: Vanilla Transformer encoder (MHSA + position-wise FFN, residual, BatchNorm1d instead of LayerNorm), shared across all channels; default small config (e.g. 3 encoder layers, D=16-128, H=4-16 depending on dataset). Flatten + linear head for forecasting; for self-supervised, head replaced by a D x P linear layer reconstructing masked patches.
pretraining_objective: Masked patch reconstruction (BERT/MAE-style, applied to time series). Series split into NON-overlapping patches; a uniformly-random subset (high ratio: 40% of patches) is set to ZERO; the Transformer reconstructs the masked patches under MSE loss. Each series keeps its own latent representation but is cross-learned via shared weights, so pretraining and downstream sets can have different numbers of channels. Fine-tuning uses two-step linear-probing-then-finetune (10 epochs LP + 20 epochs FT).
key_results: On 8 standard long-term forecasting benchmarks (Weather, Traffic, Electricity, ILI, ETTh1/h2/m1/m2) with horizons T in {96,192,336,720} (ILI {24,36,48,60}): supervised PatchTST/64 (look-back 512, 64 patches) gives an overall 21.0% MSE / 16.7% MAE reduction vs the best Transformer baseline (FEDformer); PatchTST/42 (look-back 336) gives 20.2% MSE / 16.4% MAE reduction. Example absolutes (T=96): Weather MSE 0.149 (FEDformer 0.238, DLinear 0.176); Traffic 0.360 (FEDformer 0.576, DLinear 0.410); Electricity 0.129 (FEDformer 0.186, DLinear 0.140). It also beats the strong linear baseline DLinear on most settings, especially large datasets. Self-supervised pretraining + fine-tuning matches or beats supervised training, and masked representations transfer across datasets (e.g. Electricity-pretrained -> other datasets) at SOTA accuracy.
baselines_compared: Transformer family: FEDformer, Autoformer, Informer, Pyraformer, LogTrans; the non-Transformer linear SOTA DLinear (Zeng et al. 2022); and the ablation baseline 'Original TST' (Zerveas et al. 2021, point-wise channel-mixing Transformer). Self-supervised vs TST / TS-TCC lineage referenced.
transferable_idea: PATCHING as a temporal tokenizer: aggregate consecutive timesteps of a time-varying feature into a fixed-length window, then linearly project that window to one D-dim token. For the topic's TIME-VARYING half, this is the single most reusable idea — it (a) gives each temporal token local semantic content instead of meaningless single timesteps, (b) collapses a long per-feature time axis into ~L/S tokens, controlling sequence length when many features each carry a history, and (c) is architecture-agnostic (the authors stress patching transfers to other models). The masked-patch self-supervised objective is the second transferable idea: a domain-appropriate pretext task to pretrain temporal tokens before supervised fusion.
limitations: Authors-stated: channel-independence ignores cross-channel/cross-feature correlations entirely; modeling cross-channel dependency 'properly' is left as future work. The flatten+linear forecasting head can become oversized when L*D and M*T are large (overfitting risk on scarce data). Implied: no categorical/static/missing-value handling; patch length & stride are fixed hyperparameters (no adaptive segmentation); a single linear projection per patch is a weak per-value encoder; results are single-split standard benchmarks.
pitfalls: 1) SCOPE MISMATCH FOR THIS TOPIC: channel-independence is the OPPOSITE of joint heterogeneous-feature tokenization — it deliberately removes cross-feature attention. PatchTST shows cross-channel mixing HURTS for homogeneous numeric channels with shared dynamics; this does NOT license dropping feature interactions in a 70+ mixed numerical+categorical+static setting where interactions are the whole point. Borrow patching, NOT channel-independence, for the topic. 2) 'Scales to 862 channels' is linear-in-M sequential univariate processing, not a wide mixed-feature token sequence — do not cite it as evidence for scaling to 70+ HETEROGENEOUS features. 3) Much of the gain is confounded with two non-patching ingredients: long look-back (L=336/512) and instance normalization (RevIN); the ablation isolates patching+CI but not RevIN's separate contribution, so attribute gains carefully. 4) The 20-21% improvement is vs Transformer baselines; the margin over DLinear (a linear model) is much smaller and setting-dependent — patching's value is partly 'a Transformer that finally matches/beats a strong linear model', which is a weaker claim than the headline. 5) Single linear patch projection is a poor numerical encoder per se; pair with PLR/periodic embeddings if per-value richness matters.
relevance_to_topic: Primary anchor for the temporal-feature-tokenization route: it supplies the patch token (the canonical way to tokenize a time-varying feature) and a masked self-supervised pretext, both directly reusable for the time-varying half of a 70+ mixed feature set; but its channel-independence and total lack of categorical/static handling mean it is a component, not a complete tokenizer, for the topic's target setting.
code_available: yes — official PyTorch repo https://github.com/yuqinie98/PatchTST (also integrated into the Time-Series-Library / tslib); well-cited and reproduced; pretrained checkpoints and self-supervised scripts provided.
